title: Erdogan Frames Israeli Operations as Direct Threat to Turkey as CHP Crisis Deepens
subhead: President Erdogan declared Israel's military campaigns in Syria and Lebanon a direct threat to Turkish security, trading sharp insults with Netanyahu, while Turkey's main opposition party descended into open rivalry between two competing leaders and the central bank held rates steady amid a slowing economy.

sections.heading: Erdogan Escalates Rhetoric Against Israel
sections.body:
President Recep Tayyip Erdogan told lawmakers in parliament that Israel's military operations in Syria and Lebanon have reached a point where they directly threaten Turkey. 'The attacks by Netanyahu and his network of murder on Lebanon and Syria have brought the issue to a point where it also threatens Turkey,' Erdogan said, adding that Ankara's security was tied to that of both countries. 'Damascus and Beirut are two sister cities of Istanbul. Turkey's security begins in Aleppo, in Damascus, and in Beirut,' he declared, according to Reuters and Greek City Times.

Erdogan also warned that Israel was leading a 'sneaky effort' to destabilise the Eastern Mediterranean, singling out Greek Cypriot ties with Israel: 'Small entities, whose ambitions far exceed their size, have boarded Israel's boat of mischief.' He threatened a 'clear and strong' response if Turkish or Turkish Cypriot rights in the Eastern Mediterranean were violated. Erdogan again compared Netanyahu to Hitler and urged world powers to rein in Israel, citing the unresolved Strait of Hormuz crisis as a warning of what unchecked aggression could produce globally.

Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu fired back, branding Erdogan an 'antisemitic tyrant' and calling him 'the last person who can preach morality to the State of Israel,' according to multiple outlets including News18, MSN, and the Cleveland Jewish News. The exchange marks a sharp escalation in an already hostile relationship between the two leaders.
sections.whyItMatters: Erdogan is recasting Israel's regional military campaigns as a direct Turkish national security concern, not just a moral or diplomatic objection. By invoking Turkey's security perimeter as extending to Aleppo, Damascus, and Beirut, he is staking a strategic claim that could shape Turkey's future posture in Syria and Lebanon — and potentially its military calculations.

sections.heading: CHP Opposition Crisis: Two Leaders, One Party
sections.body:
Turkey's main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP) descended into open factional conflict on Tuesday, with ousted chairman Ozgur Ozel and court-reinstated former leader Kemal Kilicdaroglu holding rival meetings in Ankara. A Turkish court last month annulled the CHP's 2023 congress that elected Ozel as chairman, citing irregularities, and reinstated Kilicdaroglu — who lost the 2023 presidential election to Erdogan. The court decision has been widely described by critics as politically motivated, Reuters reports.

Ozel, speaking to CHP lawmakers in parliament, urged resistance: 'The point is not to give up, not to surrender, but to resist.' He said the real aim was 'eliminating the CHP entirely — along with its candidate and leadership — and sustaining Erdogan's rule.' Around two-thirds of the CHP's 138 deputies voted to keep Ozel as head of the parliamentary group after the court ruling, according to Reuters.

Kilicdaroglu, speaking at CHP headquarters, promised to 'cleanse the party of dirt' and remove those involved in wrongdoing, referencing corruption cases against CHP-run municipalities. The CHP has faced an unprecedented judicial crackdown since 2024, with hundreds of members and elected officials detained on corruption charges the party denies. The government says the courts are independent.

The crisis could boost Erdogan's prospects of extending his rule beyond 2028. Analysts say early elections remain possible if the government seeks to capitalise on the opposition's disarray, Reuters notes.
sections.whyItMatters: A divided CHP weakens the only opposition force polling near parity with the AKP. If the split persists, Erdogan gains a clearer path in any snap election — and the court-anchored removal of an elected opposition leader sets a precedent for judicial intervention in party politics.

sections.heading: Central Bank Holds Rates as Economy Slows
sections.body:
The Central Bank of the Republic of Turkey (CBRT) kept its policy rate unchanged at 37% on June 10, as widely expected, citing persistent inflation and geopolitical uncertainty. Bloomberg reported that the slowing economy gave the bank cover to hold again, while ING's monitoring note described a clear loss of momentum in domestic demand alongside a widening current account deficit.

ING's analysis highlights several pressure points: annual inflation remains slightly below 30% in their forecast, with food inflation and oil price volatility from the Strait of Hormuz disruption posing upside risks. The CBRT has additionally tightened macroprudential rules — reducing loan growth caps, particularly for consumer overdraft accounts (from 2% to 1%) and SME loans (from 5% to 4.5%). ING notes this macroprudential tightening reduces the probability of any policy rate change at the June meeting but warns that upward adjustment from 37% toward the effective funding rate of around 40% remains possible if conditions deteriorate.

The lira has stabilised after May pressure, with ING projecting USD/TRY at 53.00 by year-end. FX reserves fell around USD 8 billion during the May sell-off, though the market quickly recovered. ING expects very limited scope for rate cuts in the second half, with the policy rate at around 35% by year-end and risks tilted toward a tighter stance.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is caught between a slowing economy and sticky inflation amplified by the Iran conflict and regional instability. The CBRT's hold signals it sees no room to ease, and macroprudential tightening suggests the real policy stance is even tighter than the headline rate. With the CHP crisis adding political risk, the lira and rates face a precarious second half.

sections.heading: NATO Ankara Summit: Erdogan Welcomes Trump, Sets Stage
sections.body:
President Erdogan said US President Donald Trump's decision to attend the July 7-8 NATO summit in Ankara is a 'valuable step' for alliance cohesion, according to Anadolu Agency and Al Arabiya. Erdogan told his parliamentary group that preparations had intensified and that global interest in the summit was significant.

Daily Sabah reports the summit — hosted at the Presidential Complex in Ankara, 22 years after the landmark Istanbul summit — will focus on translating the 5% GDP defense spending target agreed at The Hague into concrete capabilities. The summit comes at a moment when NATO faces a war in Europe, Middle East instability from the US-Iran conflict, and internal friction over burden-sharing. A CSIS analysis titled 'NATO 3.0 in Practice' frames the Ankara summit as a test of whether allies can update NATO's founding bargain for a more contested world, with the US pushing Europe toward greater defense self-reliance.

Foreign Affairs published a major analysis arguing Turkey is undergoing a 'quiet realignment' back toward its Western partners after years of strategic autonomy rhetoric. Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan described transatlantic ties as a 'strategic necessity' and called the summit a 'historic opportunity' to reaffirm NATO unity, according to the Foreign Affairs piece. The article traces Turkey's pivot from Moscow — reducing Russian energy dependence and pruning defense ties — as opening the door to deeper NATO cooperation just as Ankara hosts the alliance's most consequential gathering in years.
sections.whyItMatters: The Ankara summit is the most important NATO gathering since The Hague and Turkey is using host diplomacy to reinforce its Western credentials. Erdogan's welcome of Trump signals Ankara wants a visible US commitment, while Turkey's own realignment — distancing from Moscow, boosting defense exports — gives it credibility as a NATO anchor in a turbulent region.

sections.heading: UAV-Launched Ballistic Missile Enters Service
sections.body:
Turkey's National Defense Ministry announced that the Roketsan UAV-230 air-to-surface ballistic missile has entered service with the Turkish Land Forces Command. According to the Jerusalem Post and the Defense Ministry briefing, the UAV-230 is a supersonic missile with a range exceeding 150 km, a 225-kg body, and a 42-kg warhead with fragment, armor-piercing, or thermobaric options. It uses GNSS-supported inertial guidance and is designed primarily for launch from Bayraktar AKINCI and AKSUNGUR drones.

The missile was first tested from an AKINCI platform in December 2022, hitting a target at 100 km. Subsequent tests extended the range to 140 km in March 2023 and 155 km in November 2024. Roketsan's general manager described the IHA-230 as 'the steel claws of our UAVs,' pairing it with the CAKIR cruise missile to extend Turkey's drone strike range.

The UAV-230's entry into service comes amid Turkey's broader defense autonomy push — Ankara aims to meet nearly all military needs domestically by 2030, with flagship projects including the KAAN fifth-generation fighter and the Altay main battle tank. The Tayfun Block-4 ballistic missile, reaching speeds above Mach 5 with an 800 km range, is planned for mass production in 2026.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is the first country to field a UAV-launched ballistic missile, extending its already formidable drone warfare capability to supersonic standoff strike ranges. This is not just a technical milestone — it changes the threat calculus for any adversary operating within 150 km of a Turkish drone, and positions Turkey as a defense exporter that practices what it sells.

sections.heading: COP31 Presidency Launches Global Electrification Target
sections.body:
COP31 co-hosts Turkey and Australia unveiled their priority agenda on June 9, with Turkish Environment Minister Murat Kurum proposing a voluntary global target for electricity to meet 35% of world energy demand by 2035, up from roughly 20% today. The Guardian, E&E News, and Reuters all reported the announcement, which also includes targets on waste reduction and building efficiency.

The electrification push is the centerpiece of Turkey's COP31 presidency and represents an attempt to rally governments behind a concrete, measurable goal rather than the more abstract pledges that have characterised recent climate summits. Climate Home News and edie.net report that Turkey and Australia are seeking formal sign-ups from governments during the negotiations.

The proposal comes at an awkward moment for Turkey domestically — the country still relies heavily on coal and imported gas, and its own energy transition faces headwinds from the Iran conflict disrupting global oil and gas markets. Still, the COP31 presidency gives Erdogan a global platform and positions Turkey as a bridge between developed and developing country positions on climate finance.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is using the COP31 presidency to stake out a leadership role in global climate diplomacy, proposing the most concrete electrification target yet discussed at a COP. Whether governments sign on will depend on the negotiation dynamics — but the target itself reframes the decarbonisation debate around electrification, which directly serves Turkey's interests as a power transit country.

sections.heading: Energy Diplomacy: Turkey-Bulgaria Gas Talks and Trans-Caspian Prospects
sections.body:
Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan confirmed that Ankara and Sofia are negotiating revisions to the BOTAS-Bulgargaz gas supply agreement, signed in 2023 after Bulgaria lost access to Russian gas. In an interview with BTA, Fidan said Turkey aims to negotiate a broader energy cooperation agreement including increased gas transmission capacity between the two countries. He noted that current capacity from Turkey to Bulgaria is insufficient for requested additional volumes and that further Bulgarian investment in infrastructure is needed, Novinite reports.

Fidan also stressed that political stability in Bulgaria matters for long-term energy projects, calling for a strong government with a long-term mandate in Sofia. Separately, RFE/RL reported renewed discussion of the long-stalled Trans-Caspian gas pipeline, with the Iran war disrupting traditional supply routes and the West seeking alternatives to Russian transit — potentially elevating Turkey's role as an energy hub.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is systematically expanding its gas transit role in southeastern Europe, using the disruption caused by the Russia-Ukraine war and the Iran conflict to position itself as an indispensable energy corridor. The BOTAS-Bulgargaz renegotiation and Trans-Caspian pipeline discussions both reflect Ankara's strategy of turning geographic position into commercial and diplomatic leverage.

bottomLine: June 10 saw Erdogan draw Turkey's security perimeter explicitly to Aleppo, Damascus, and Beirut — framing Israel's regional campaigns as a direct national threat while trading venomous personal attacks with Netanyahu. At home, the CHP's institutional crisis deepened with two leaders holding rival meetings, a division that could reshape Turkish electoral politics ahead of possible snap elections. The central bank held rates at 37% as the economy slows and inflation stays sticky, while the UAV-230 ballistic missile's entry into service extends Turkey's drone warfare reach to 150+ km. On the diplomatic front, Erdogan welcomed Trump to the July NATO summit in Ankara, and the COP31 presidency launched a global electrification push. Turkey is simultaneously projecting power outward — defense, energy transit, climate diplomacy — while its domestic opposition implodes.
sources: Reuters, US News & World Report, Greek City Times, News18, MSN, Cleveland Jewish News, ING Think, Bloomberg, Daily Sabah, Anadolu Agency, Al Arabiya English, CSIS, Foreign Affairs, Jerusalem Post, Guardian, E&E News, Climate Home News, edie.net, Novinite, RFE/RL
